38	The fellowship of the hedge had not survived the night, it was plain to see. Ser Glendon kept his own company, prickly and sullen. Kyle the Cat judged that it would be midday before they were allowed to board the ferry, so he detached himself from the others to try to ingratiate himself with Lord Smallwood, with whom he had some slight acquaintance. Ser Maynard spent his time gossiping with the innkeep.
39	
40	"Stay well away from that one," Dunk warned Egg. There was something about Plumm that troubled him. "He could be a robber knight, for all we know."
41	
42	The warning only seemed to make Ser Maynard more interesting to Egg. "I never knew a robber knight. Do you think he means to rob the dragon's egg?"
43	
44	"Lord Butterwell will have the egg well guarded, I'm sure." Dunk scratched the midge bites on his neck. "Do you think he might display it at the feast? I'd like to get a look at one."
45	
46	"I'd show you mine, ser, but it's at Summerhall."
47	
48	"Yours? Your dragon's egg?" Dunk frowned down at the boy, wondering if this was some jape.
49	
50	"Where did it come from?"
51	
52	"From a dragon, ser. They put it in my cradle."
53	
54	"Do you want a clout in the ear? There are no dragons."
55	
56	"No, but there are eggs. The last dragon left a clutch of five, and they have more on Dragonstone, old ones from before the Dance. My brothers all have them too. Aerion's looks as though it's made of gold and silver, with veins of fire running through it. Mine is white and green, all swirly."
57	
58	"Your dragon's egg." They put it in his cradle. Dunk was so used to Egg that sometimes he forgot Aegon was a prince. Of course they'd put a dragon egg inside his cradle."Well, see that you don't go mentioning this egg where anyone is like to hear."
59	
60	"I'm not stupid, ser." Egg lowered his voice. "Someday the dragons will return. My brother Daeron's dreamed of it, and King Aerys read it in a prophecy. Maybe it will be my egg that hatches. That would be splendid."
61	
62	"Would it?" Dunk had his doubts.
63	
64	Not Egg. "Aemon and I used to pretend that our eggs would be the ones to hatch. If they did, we could fly through the sky on dragonback, like the first Aegon and his sisters."
65	
66	"Aye, and if all the other knights in the realm should die, I'd be the Lord Commander of the Kingsguard. If these eggs are so bloody precious, why is Lord Butterwell giving his away?"
67	
68	"To show the realm how rich he is?"
69	
70	"I suppose." Dunk scratched his neck again and glanced over at Ser Glendon Ball, who was tightening the cinches on his saddle as he waited for the ferry. That horse will never serve. Ser Glendon's mount was a sway-backed stot, undersized and old. "What do you know about his sire? Why did they call him Fireball?"
71	
72	"For his hot head and red hair. Ser Quentyn Ball was the master-at-arms at the Red Keep. He taught my father and my uncles how to fight. The Great Bastards too. King Aegon promised to raise him to the Kingsguard, so Fireball made his wife join the silent sisters, only by the time a place came open, King Aegon was dead and King Daeron named Ser Willam Wylde instead. My father says that it was Fireball as much as Bittersteel who convinced Daemon Blackfyre to claim the crown, and rescued him when Daeron sent the Kingsguard to arrest him. Later on, Fireball killed Lord Lefford at the gates of Lannisport and sent the Grey Lion running back to hide inside the Rock. At the crossing of the Mandel, he cut down the sons of Lady Penrose one by one.
73	
74	They say he spared the life of the youngest one as a kindness to his mother."
75	
76	"That was chivalrous of him," Dunk had to admit. "Did Ser Quentyn die upon the Redgrass Field?"
77	
78	"Before, ser," Egg replied. "An archer put an arrow through his throat as he dismounted by a stream to have a drink. Just some common man, no one knows who."
79	
80	"Those common men can be dangerous when they get it in their heads to start slaying lords and heroes." Dunk saw the ferry creeping slowly across the lake. "Here it comes."
81	
82	"It's slow. Are we going to go to Whitewalls, ser?"
83	
84	"Why not? I want to see this dragon's egg." Dunk smiled. "If I win the tourney, we'd both have dragon's eggs."
85	
86	Egg gave him a doubtful look.
87	
88	"What? Why are you looking at me that way?"
89	
90	"I could tell you, ser," the boy said solemnly, "but I need to learn to hold my tongue."
91	
92	They seated the hedge knights well below the salt, closer to the doors than to the dais.
93	
94	Whitewalls was almost new as castles went, having been raised a mere forty years ago by the grandsire of its present lord. The smallfolk hereabouts called it the Milk house, for its walls and keeps and towers were made of finely dressed white stone, quarried in the Vale and brought over the mountains at great expense. Inside were floors and pillars of milky white marble veined with gold; the rafters overhead were carved from the bone-pale trunks of weirwoods.
95	
96	Dunk could not begin to imagine what all of that had cost.
97	
98	The hall was not so large as some others he had known, though. At least we were allowed beneath the roof, Dunk thought as he took his place on the bench between Ser Maynard Plumm and Kyle the Cat. Though uninvited, the three of them had been welcomed to the feast quick enough; it was ill luck to refuse a knight hospitality on your wedding day.
99	
100	Young Ser Glendon had a harder time, however. "Fireball never had a son," Dunk heard Lord Butterwell's steward tell him, loudly. The stripling answered heatedly, and the name of Ser Morgan Dunstable was mentioned several times, but the steward had remained adamant.
101	
102	When Ser Glendon touched his sword hilt, a dozen men-at-arms appeared with spears in hand, but for a moment it looked as though there might be bloodshed. It was only the intervention of a big blond knight named Kirby Pimm that saved the situation. Dunk was too far away to hear, but he saw Pimm clasp an arm around the steward's shoulders and murmur in his ear, laughing.
103	
104	The steward frowned, and said something to Ser Glendon that turned the boy's face dark red.
105	
106	He looks as if he's about to cry, Dunk thought, watching. That, or kill someone. After all of that, the young knight was finally admitted to the castle hall.
107	
108	Poor Egg was not so fortunate. "The great hall is for the lords and knights," an understeward had informed them haughtily when Dunk tried to bring the boy inside. "We have set up tables in the inner yard for squires, grooms, and men-at-arms."
109	
110	If you had an inkling who he was, you would seat him on the dais on a cushioned throne. Dunk had not much liked the look of the other squires. A few were lads of Egg's own age, but most were older, seasoned fighters who long ago had made the choice to serve a knight rather than become one. Or did they have a choice? Knighthood required more than chivalry and skill at arms; it required horse and sword and armor too, and all of that was costly. "Watch your tongue," he told Egg before he left him in that company. "These are grown men; they won't take kindly to your insolence. Sit and eat and listen, might be you'll learn some things."
111	
112	For his own part, Dunk was just glad to be out of the hot sun, with a wine cup before him and a chance to fill his belly. Even a hedge knight grows weary of chewing every bite of food for half an hour. Down here below the salt, the fare would be more plain than fancy, but there would be no lack of it. Below the salt was good enough for Dunk.
113	
114	But peasant's pride is lordling's shame, the old man used to say. "This cannot be my proper place," Ser Glendon Ball told the understeward hotly. He had donned a clean doublet for the feast, a handsome old garment with gold lace at the cuffs and collar and the red chevron and white plates of House Ball sewn across the chest. "Do you know who my father was?"
115	
116	"A noble knight and mighty lord, I have no doubt," said the understeward, "but the same is true of many here. Please take your seat or take your leave, ser. It is all the same to me."
117	
118	In the end, the boy took his place below the salt with the rest of them, his mouth sullen. The long white hall was filling up as more knights crowded onto the benches. The crowd was larger than Dunk had anticipated, and from the looks of it, some of the guests had come a very long way. He and Egg had not been around so many lords and knights since Ashford Meadow, and there was no way to guess who else might turn up next. We should have stayed out in the hedges, sleeping under trees. If I am recognized ...
119	
120	When a serving man placed a loaf of black bread on the cloth in front of each of them, Dunk was grateful for the distraction. He sawed the loaf open lengthwise, hollowed out the bottom half for a trencher, and ate the top. It was stale, but compared with his salt beef, it was custard.
121	
122	At least it did not have to be soaked in ale or milk or water to make it soft enough to chew.
123	
124	"Ser Duncan, you appear to be attracting a deal of attention," Ser Maynard Plumm observed as Lord Vyrwel and his party went parading past them toward places of high honor at the top of the hall. "Those girls up on the dais cannot seem to take their eyes off you. I'll wager they have never seen a man so big. Even seated, you are half a head taller than any man in the hall."
125	
126	Dunk hunched his shoulders. He was used to being stared at, but that did not mean he liked it.
127	
128	"Let them look."
129	
130	"That's the Old Ox down there beneath the dais," Ser Maynard said. "They call him a huge man, but seems to me his belly is the biggest thing about him. You're a bloody giant next to him."
131	
132	"Indeed, ser," said one of their companions on the bench, a sallow man, saturnine, clad in grey and green. His eyes were small and shrewd, set close together beneath thin arching brows. A neat black beard framed his mouth, to make up for his receding hair. "In such a field as this, your size alone should make you one of the most formidable competitors."
133	
134	"I had heard the Brute of Bracken might be coming," said another man, farther down the bench.
135	
136	"I think not," said the man in green and grey. "This is only a bit of jousting to celebrate His Lordship's nuptials. A tilt in the yard to mark the tilt between the sheets. Hardly worth the bother for the likes of Otho Bracken." Ser Kyle the Cat took a drink of wine. "I'll wager my lord of Butterwell does not take the field either. He will cheer on his champions from his lord's box in the shade."
137	
138	"Then he'll see his champions fall," boasted Ser Glendon Ball, "and in the end, he'll hand his egg to me."
139	
140	"Ser Glendon is the son of Fireball," Ser Kyle explained to the new man. "Might we have the honor of your name, ser?"
141	
142	"Ser Uthor Underleaf. The son of no one of importance." Underleafs garments were of good cloth, clean and well cared for, but simply cut. A silver clasp in the shape of a snail fastened his cloak. "If your lance is the equal of your tongue, Ser Glendon, you may even give this big fellow here a contest."
143	
144	Ser Glendon glanced at Dunk as the wine was being poured. "If we meet, he'll fall. I don't care how big he is."
145	
146	Dunk watched a server fill his wine cup. "I am better with a sword than with a lance," he admitted, "and even better with a battleaxe. Will there be a melee here?" His size and strength would stand him in good stead in a melee, and he knew he could give as good as he got.
147	
148	Jousting was another matter.
149	
150	"A melee? At a marriage?" Ser Kyle sounded shocked. "That would be unseemly."
151	
152	Ser Maynard gave a chuckle. "A marriage is a melee, as any married man could tell you."
153	
154	Ser Uthor chuckled. "There's just the joust, I fear, but besides the dragon's egg, Lord Butterwell has promised thirty golden dragons for the loser of the final tilt, and ten each for the knights defeated in the round before."
155	
156	Ten dragons is not so bad. Ten dragons would buy a palfrey, so Dunk would not need to ride Thunder save in battle. Ten dragons would buy a suit of plate for Egg, and a proper knight's pavilion sewn with Dunk's tree and falling star. Ten dragons would mean roast goose and ham and pigeon pie.
157	
158	"There are ransoms to be had as well, for those who win their matches," Ser Uthor said as he hollowed out his trencher, "and I have heard it rumored that some men place wagers on the tilts. Lord Butterwell himself is not fond of taking risks, but amongst his guests are some who wager heavily."
159	
160	No sooner had he spoken than Ambrose Butterwell made his entrance, to a fanfare of trumpets from the minstrel's gallery. Dunk shoved to his feet with the rest as Butterwell escorted his new bride down a patterned Myrish carpet to the dais, arm in arm. The girl was fifteen and freshly flowered, her lord husband fifty and freshly widowed. She was pink and he was grey. Her bride's cloak trailed behind her, done in candy green and white and yellow. It looked so hot and heavy that Dunk wondered how she could bear to wear it. Lord Butterwell looked hot and heavy too, with his heavy jowls and thinning flaxen hair.
161	
162	The bride's father followed close behind her, hand in hand with his young son. Lord Frey of the Crossing was a lean man elegant in blue and grey, his heir a chinless boy of four whose nose was dripping snot. Lords Costayne and Risley came next, with their lady wives, daughters of Lord Butterwell by his first wife. Frey's daughters followed with their own husbands. Then came Lord Gormon Peake; Lords Smallwood, and Shawney; various lesser lords and landed knights. Amongst them Dunk glimpsed John the Fiddler and Alyn Cockshaw. Lord Alyn looked to be in his cups, though the feast had not yet properly begun.
163	
164	By the time all of them had sauntered to the dais, the high table was as crowded as the benches. Lord Butterwell and his bride sat on plump downy cushions in a double throne of gilded oak. The rest planted themselves in tall chairs with fancifully carved arms. On the wall behind them, two huge banners hung from the rafters: the twin towers of Frey, blue on grey, and the green and white and yellow undy of the Butterwells.
165	
166	It fell to Lord Frey to lead the toasts. "The king!" he began simply. Ser Glendon held his wine cup out above the water basin. Dunk clanked his cup against it, and against Ser Uthor's and the rest as well. They drank.
167	
168	" Lord Butterwell, our gracious host," Frey proclaimed next. "May the Father grant him long life and many sons."
169	
170	They drank again.
171	
172	" Lady Butterwell, the maiden brick, my darling daughter. May the Mother make her fertile."
173	
174	Frey gave the girl a smile. "I shall want a grandson before the year is out. Twins would suit me even better, so churn the butter well tonight, my sweet."
175	
176	Laughter rang against the rafters, and the guests drank still once more. The wine was rich and red and sweet.
177	
178	Then Lord Frey said, "I give you the King's Hand, Brynden Rivers. May the Crone's lamp light his path to wisdom." He lifted his goblet high and drank, together with Lord Butterwell and his bride and the others on the dais. Below the salt, Ser Glendon turned his cup over to spill its contents to the floor.
179	
180	"A sad waste of good wine," said Maynard Plumm.
181	
182	"I do not drink to kinslayers," said Ser Glendon. "Lord Bloodraven is a sorcerer and a bastard."
183	
184	"Born bastard," Ser Uthor agreed mildly, "but his royal father made him legitimate as he lay dying." He drank deep, as did Ser Maynard and many others in the hall. Near as many lowered their cups, or turned them upside down as Ball had done. Dunk's own cup was heavy in his hand. How many eyes does Lord Bloodraven have? the riddle went. A thousand eyes, and one.
185	
186	Toast followed toast, some proposed by Lord Frey and some by others. They drank to young Lord Tully, Lord Butterwell's liege lord, who had begged off from the wedding. They drank to the health of Leo Longthorn, Lord of Highgarden, who was rumored to be ailing. They drank to the memory of their gallant dead. Aye, thought Dunk, remembering. I'll gladly drink to them.
187	
188	Ser John the Fiddler proposed the final toast. " To my brave brothers! I know that they are smiling tonight!"
189	
190	Dunk had not intended to drink so much, with the jousting on the morrow, but the cups were filled anew after every toast, and he found he had a thirst. "Never refuse a cup of wine or a horn of ale," Ser Arlan had once told him, "it may be a year before you see another." It would have been discourteous not to toast the bride and groom, he told himself, and dangerous not to drink to the king and his Hand, with strangers all about.
191	
192	Mercifully, the Fiddler's toast was the last. Lord Butterwell rose ponderously to thank them for coming and promise good jousting on the morrow. "Let the feast begin!"
193	
194	Suckling pig was served at the high table, a peacock roasted in its plumage, a great pike crusted with crushed almonds. Not a bite of that made it down below the salt. Instead of suckling pig, they got salt pork, soaked in almond milk and peppered pleasantly. In place of peacock, they had capons, crisped up nice and brown and stuffed with onions, herbs, mushrooms, and roasted chestnuts. In place of pike, they ate chunks of flaky white cod in a pastry coffyn, with some sort of tasty brown sauce that Dunk could not quite place. There was pease porridge besides, buttered turnips, carrots drizzled with honey, and a ripe white cheese that smelled as strong as Bennis of the Brown Shield. Dunk ate well, but all the while wondered what Egg was getting in the yard. Just in case, he slipped half a capon into the pocket of his cloak, with some hunks of bread and a little of the smelly cheese.
195	
196	As they ate, pipes and fiddles filled the air with spritely tunes, and the talk turned to the morrow's jousting. "Ser Franklyn Frey is well regarded along the Green Fork," said Uthor Underleaf, who seemed to know these local heroes well. "That's him upon the dais, the uncle of the bride. Lucas Nayland is down from Flag's Mire, he should not be discounted. Nor should Ser Mortimer Boggs, of Crackclaw Point. Elsewise, this should be a tourney of household knights and village heroes. Kirby Pimm and Galtry the Green are the best of those, though neither is a match for Lord Butterwell's good-son, Black Tom Heddle. A nasty bit of business, that one. He won the hand of His Lordship's eldest daughter by killing three of her other suitors, it's said, and once unhorsed the Lord of Casterly Rock."
197	
198	"What, young Lord Tybolt?" asked Ser Maynard.
199	
200	"No, the old Grey Lion, the one who died in the spring." That was how men spoke of those who had perished during the Great Spring Sickness. He died in the spring. Tens of thousands had died in the spring, among them a king and two young princes.
201	
202	"Do not slight Ser Buford Bulwer," said Kyle the Cat. "The Old Ox slew forty men upon the Redgrass Field."
203	
204	"And every year his count grows higher," said Ser Maynard. "Bulwer's day is done. Look at him.
205	
206	Past sixty, soft and fat, and his right eye is good as blind."
207	
208	"Do not trouble to search the hall for the champion," a voice behind Dunk said. "Here I stand, sers. Feast your eyes."
209	
210	Dunk turned to find Ser John the Fiddler looming over him, a half smile on his lips. His white silk doublet had lagged sleeves lined with red satin, so long their points drooped down past his knees. A heavy silver chain looped across his chest, studded with huge dark amethysts whose color matched his eyes. That chain is worth as much as everything I own, Dunk thought.
211	
212	The wine had colored Ser Glendon's cheeks and inflamed his pimples. "Who are you, to make such boasts?"
213	
214	"They call me John the Fiddler."
215	
216	"Are you a musician or a warrior?"
217	
218	"I can make sweet song with either lance or resined bow, as it happens. Every wedding needs a singer, and every tourney needs a mystery knight. May I join you? Butterwell was good enough to place me on the dais, but I prefer the company of my fellow hedge knights to fat pink ladies and old men." The Fiddler clapped Dunk upon the shoulder. "Be a good fellow and shove over, Ser Duncan."
219	
220	Dunk shoved over. "You are too late for food, ser."
221	
222	"No matter. I know where Butterwell's kitchens are. There is still some wine, I trust?" The Fiddler smelled of oranges and limes, with a hint of some strange eastern spice beneath.
223	
224	Nutmeg, perhaps. Dunk could not have said. What did he know of nutmeg?
225	
226	"Your boasting is unseemly," Ser Glendon told the Fiddler.
227	
228	"Truly? Then I must beg for your forgiveness, ser. I would never wish to give offense to any son of Fireball."
229	
230	That took the youth aback. "You know who I am?"
231	
232	"Your father's son, I hope."
233	
234	"Look," said Ser Kyle the Cat. "The wedding pie."
235	
236	Six kitchen boys were pushing it through the doors, upon a wide wheeled cart. The pie was brown and crusty and immense, and there were noises coming from inside it, squeaks and squawks and thumps. Lord and Lady Butterwell descended from the dais to meet it, sword in hand. When they cut it open, half a hundred birds burst forth to fly around the hall. In other wedding feasts Dunk had attended, the pies had been filled with doves or songbirds, but inside this one were bluejays and skylarks, pigeons and doves, mockingbirds and nightingales, small brown sparrows and a great red parrot. "One-and-twenty sorts of birds," said Ser Kyle.
237	
238	"One-and-twenty sorts of bird droppings," said Ser Maynard.
239	
240	"You have no poetry in your heart, ser."
241	
242	"You have shit upon your shoulder."
243	
244	"This is the proper way to fill a pie," Ser Kyle sniffed, cleaning off his tunic. "The pie is meant to be the marriage, and a true marriage has in it many sorts of things. . .joy and grief, pain and pleasure, love and lust and loyalty. So it is fitting that there be birds of many sorts. No man ever truly knows what a new wife will bring him."
245	
246	"Her cunt," said Plumm, "or what would be the point?"
247	
248	Dunk shoved back from the table. "I need a breath of air." It was a piss he needed, truth be told, but in fine company like this, it was more courteous to talk of air. "Pray excuse me."
249	
250	"Flurry back, ser," said the Fiddler. "There are jugglers yet to come, and you do not want to miss the bedding."
251

file.content:
---
book: TMK
chapter_number: 1
pov_character: Dunk
real_identity: Duncan the Tall
pov_chapter_number: 1
pov_label: "Dunk I"
collection: tales-of-dunk-and-egg
first_available: pre-agot
file_name: tmk-dunk-01-p03.md
unit_part: "3 of 9"
part_of: tmk-dunk-01
scene_label: "Ferry crossing and Whitewalls wedding feast"
---

The next day proved the truth of Ser Kyle's prophecy. Ned's ferry was nowise large enough to accomodate all those who wished to cross, so Lords Costayne and Shawney must go first, with their tails. That required several trips, each taking more than an hour. There were the mudflats to contend with, horses and wagons to be gotten down the planks, loaded on the boat, and unloaded again across the lake. The two lords slowed matters even further when they got into a shouting match over precedence. Shawney was the elder, but Costayne held himself to be better born.

There was nought that Dunk could do but wait and swelter. "We could go first if you let me use my boot," Egg said.

"We could," Dunk answered, "but we won't. Lord Costayne and Lord Shawney were here before us. Besides, they're lords."

Egg made a face. "Rebel lords."

Dunk frowned down at him. "What do you mean?"

"They were for the Black Dragon. Well, Lord Shawney was, and Lord Costayne's father. Aemon and I used to fight the battle on Maester Melaquin's green table with painted soldiers and little banners. Costayne's arms quarter a silver chalice on black with a black rose on gold. That banner was on the left of Daemon's host. Shawney was with Bittersteel on the right, and almost died of his wounds."

"Old dead history. They're here now, aren't they? So they bent the knee, and King Daeron gave them pardon."

"Yes, but. . ."

Dunk pinched the boy's lips shut. "Hold your tongue."

Egg held his tongue.

No sooner had the last boatload of Shawney men pushed off than Lord and Lady Smallwood turned up at the landing with their own tail, so they must needs wait again.

The fellowship of the hedge had not survived the night, it was plain to see. Ser Glendon kept his own company, prickly and sullen. Kyle the Cat judged that it would be midday before they were allowed to board the ferry, so he detached himself from the others to try to ingratiate himself with Lord Smallwood, with whom he had some slight acquaintance. Ser Maynard spent his time gossiping with the innkeep.

"Stay well away from that one," Dunk warned Egg. There was something about Plumm that troubled him. "He could be a robber knight, for all we know."

The warning only seemed to make Ser Maynard more interesting to Egg. "I never knew a robber knight. Do you think he means to rob the dragon's egg?"

"Lord Butterwell will have the egg well guarded, I'm sure." Dunk scratched the midge bites on his neck. "Do you think he might display it at the feast? I'd like to get a look at one."

"I'd show you mine, ser, but it's at Summerhall."

"Yours? Your dragon's egg?" Dunk frowned down at the boy, wondering if this was some jape.

"Where did it come from?"

"From a dragon, ser. They put it in my cradle."

"Do you want a clout in the ear? There are no dragons."

"No, but there are eggs. The last dragon left a clutch of five, and they have more on Dragonstone, old ones from before the Dance. My brothers all have them too. Aerion's looks as though it's made of gold and silver, with veins of fire running through it. Mine is white and green, all swirly."

"Your dragon's egg." They put it in his cradle. Dunk was so used to Egg that sometimes he forgot Aegon was a prince. Of course they'd put a dragon egg inside his cradle."Well, see that you don't go mentioning this egg where anyone is like to hear."

"I'm not stupid, ser." Egg lowered his voice. "Someday the dragons will return. My brother Daeron's dreamed of it, and King Aerys read it in a prophecy. Maybe it will be my egg that hatches. That would be splendid."

"Would it?" Dunk had his doubts.

Not Egg. "Aemon and I used to pretend that our eggs would be the ones to hatch. If they did, we could fly through the sky on dragonback, like the first Aegon and his sisters."

"Aye, and if all the other knights in the realm should die, I'd be the Lord Commander of the Kingsguard. If these eggs are so bloody precious, why is Lord Butterwell giving his away?"

"To show the realm how rich he is?"

"I suppose." Dunk scratched his neck again and glanced over at Ser Glendon Ball, who was tightening the cinches on his saddle as he waited for the ferry. That horse will never serve. Ser Glendon's mount was a sway-backed stot, undersized and old. "What do you know about his sire? Why did they call him Fireball?"

"For his hot head and red hair. Ser Quentyn Ball was the master-at-arms at the Red Keep. He taught my father and my uncles how to fight. The Great Bastards too. King Aegon promised to raise him to the Kingsguard, so Fireball made his wife join the silent sisters, only by the time a place came open, King Aegon was dead and King Daeron named Ser Willam Wylde instead. My father says that it was Fireball as much as Bittersteel who convinced Daemon Blackfyre to claim the crown, and rescued him when Daeron sent the Kingsguard to arrest him. Later on, Fireball killed Lord Lefford at the gates of Lannisport and sent the Grey Lion running back to hide inside the Rock. At the crossing of the Mandel, he cut down the sons of Lady Penrose one by one.

They say he spared the life of the youngest one as a kindness to his mother."

"That was chivalrous of him," Dunk had to admit. "Did Ser Quentyn die upon the Redgrass Field?"

"Before, ser," Egg replied. "An archer put an arrow through his throat as he dismounted by a stream to have a drink. Just some common man, no one knows who."

"Those common men can be dangerous when they get it in their heads to start slaying lords and heroes." Dunk saw the ferry creeping slowly across the lake. "Here it comes."

"It's slow. Are we going to go to Whitewalls, ser?"

"Why not? I want to see this dragon's egg." Dunk smiled. "If I win the tourney, we'd both have dragon's eggs."

Egg gave him a doubtful look.

"What? Why are you looking at me that way?"

"I could tell you, ser," the boy said solemnly, "but I need to learn to hold my tongue."

They seated the hedge knights well below the salt, closer to the doors than to the dais.

Whitewalls was almost new as castles went, having been raised a mere forty years ago by the grandsire of its present lord. The smallfolk hereabouts called it the Milk house, for its walls and keeps and towers were made of finely dressed white stone, quarried in the Vale and brought over the mountains at great expense. Inside were floors and pillars of milky white marble veined with gold; the rafters overhead were carved from the bone-pale trunks of weirwoods.

Dunk could not begin to imagine what all of that had cost.

The hall was not so large as some others he had known, though. At least we were allowed beneath the roof, Dunk thought as he took his place on the bench between Ser Maynard Plumm and Kyle the Cat. Though uninvited, the three of them had been welcomed to the feast quick enough; it was ill luck to refuse a knight hospitality on your wedding day.

Young Ser Glendon had a harder time, however. "Fireball never had a son," Dunk heard Lord Butterwell's steward tell him, loudly. The stripling answered heatedly, and the name of Ser Morgan Dunstable was mentioned several times, but the steward had remained adamant.

When Ser Glendon touched his sword hilt, a dozen men-at-arms appeared with spears in hand, but for a moment it looked as though there might be bloodshed. It was only the intervention of a big blond knight named Kirby Pimm that saved the situation. Dunk was too far away to hear, but he saw Pimm clasp an arm around the steward's shoulders and murmur in his ear, laughing.

The steward frowned, and said something to Ser Glendon that turned the boy's face dark red.

He looks as if he's about to cry, Dunk thought, watching. That, or kill someone. After all of that, the young knight was finally admitted to the castle hall.

Poor Egg was not so fortunate. "The great hall is for the lords and knights," an understeward had informed them haughtily when Dunk tried to bring the boy inside. "We have set up tables in the inner yard for squires, grooms, and men-at-arms."

If you had an inkling who he was, you would seat him on the dais on a cushioned throne. Dunk had not much liked the look of the other squires. A few were lads of Egg's own age, but most were older, seasoned fighters who long ago had made the choice to serve a knight rather than become one. Or did they have a choice? Knighthood required more than chivalry and skill at arms; it required horse and sword and armor too, and all of that was costly. "Watch your tongue," he told Egg before he left him in that company. "These are grown men; they won't take kindly to your insolence. Sit and eat and listen, might be you'll learn some things."

For his own part, Dunk was just glad to be out of the hot sun, with a wine cup before him and a chance to fill his belly. Even a hedge knight grows weary of chewing every bite of food for half an hour. Down here below the salt, the fare would be more plain than fancy, but there would be no lack of it. Below the salt was good enough for Dunk.

But peasant's pride is lordling's shame, the old man used to say. "This cannot be my proper place," Ser Glendon Ball told the understeward hotly. He had donned a clean doublet for the feast, a handsome old garment with gold lace at the cuffs and collar and the red chevron and white plates of House Ball sewn across the chest. "Do you know who my father was?"

"A noble knight and mighty lord, I have no doubt," said the understeward, "but the same is true of many here. Please take your seat or take your leave, ser. It is all the same to me."

In the end, the boy took his place below the salt with the rest of them, his mouth sullen. The long white hall was filling up as more knights crowded onto the benches. The crowd was larger than Dunk had anticipated, and from the looks of it, some of the guests had come a very long way. He and Egg had not been around so many lords and knights since Ashford Meadow, and there was no way to guess who else might turn up next. We should have stayed out in the hedges, sleeping under trees. If I am recognized ...

When a serving man placed a loaf of black bread on the cloth in front of each of them, Dunk was grateful for the distraction. He sawed the loaf open lengthwise, hollowed out the bottom half for a trencher, and ate the top. It was stale, but compared with his salt beef, it was custard.

At least it did not have to be soaked in ale or milk or water to make it soft enough to chew.

"Ser Duncan, you appear to be attracting a deal of attention," Ser Maynard Plumm observed as Lord Vyrwel and his party went parading past them toward places of high honor at the top of the hall. "Those girls up on the dais cannot seem to take their eyes off you. I'll wager they have never seen a man so big. Even seated, you are half a head taller than any man in the hall."

Dunk hunched his shoulders. He was used to being stared at, but that did not mean he liked it.

"Let them look."

"That's the Old Ox down there beneath the dais," Ser Maynard said. "They call him a huge man, but seems to me his belly is the biggest thing about him. You're a bloody giant next to him."

"Indeed, ser," said one of their companions on the bench, a sallow man, saturnine, clad in grey and green. His eyes were small and shrewd, set close together beneath thin arching brows. A neat black beard framed his mouth, to make up for his receding hair. "In such a field as this, your size alone should make you one of the most formidable competitors."

"I had heard the Brute of Bracken might be coming," said another man, farther down the bench.

"I think not," said the man in green and grey. "This is only a bit of jousting to celebrate His Lordship's nuptials. A tilt in the yard to mark the tilt between the sheets. Hardly worth the bother for the likes of Otho Bracken." Ser Kyle the Cat took a drink of wine. "I'll wager my lord of Butterwell does not take the field either. He will cheer on his champions from his lord's box in the shade."

"Then he'll see his champions fall," boasted Ser Glendon Ball, "and in the end, he'll hand his egg to me."

"Ser Glendon is the son of Fireball," Ser Kyle explained to the new man. "Might we have the honor of your name, ser?"

"Ser Uthor Underleaf. The son of no one of importance." Underleafs garments were of good cloth, clean and well cared for, but simply cut. A silver clasp in the shape of a snail fastened his cloak. "If your lance is the equal of your tongue, Ser Glendon, you may even give this big fellow here a contest."

Ser Glendon glanced at Dunk as the wine was being poured. "If we meet, he'll fall. I don't care how big he is."

Dunk watched a server fill his wine cup. "I am better with a sword than with a lance," he admitted, "and even better with a battleaxe. Will there be a melee here?" His size and strength would stand him in good stead in a melee, and he knew he could give as good as he got.

Jousting was another matter.

"A melee? At a marriage?" Ser Kyle sounded shocked. "That would be unseemly."

Ser Maynard gave a chuckle. "A marriage is a melee, as any married man could tell you."

Ser Uthor chuckled. "There's just the joust, I fear, but besides the dragon's egg, Lord Butterwell has promised thirty golden dragons for the loser of the final tilt, and ten each for the knights defeated in the round before."

Ten dragons is not so bad. Ten dragons would buy a palfrey, so Dunk would not need to ride Thunder save in battle. Ten dragons would buy a suit of plate for Egg, and a proper knight's pavilion sewn with Dunk's tree and falling star. Ten dragons would mean roast goose and ham and pigeon pie.

"There are ransoms to be had as well, for those who win their matches," Ser Uthor said as he hollowed out his trencher, "and I have heard it rumored that some men place wagers on the tilts. Lord Butterwell himself is not fond of taking risks, but amongst his guests are some who wager heavily."

No sooner had he spoken than Ambrose Butterwell made his entrance, to a fanfare of trumpets from the minstrel's gallery. Dunk shoved to his feet with the rest as Butterwell escorted his new bride down a patterned Myrish carpet to the dais, arm in arm. The girl was fifteen and freshly flowered, her lord husband fifty and freshly widowed. She was pink and he was grey. Her bride's cloak trailed behind her, done in candy green and white and yellow. It looked so hot and heavy that Dunk wondered how she could bear to wear it. Lord Butterwell looked hot and heavy too, with his heavy jowls and thinning flaxen hair.

The bride's father followed close behind her, hand in hand with his young son. Lord Frey of the Crossing was a lean man elegant in blue and grey, his heir a chinless boy of four whose nose was dripping snot. Lords Costayne and Risley came next, with their lady wives, daughters of Lord Butterwell by his first wife. Frey's daughters followed with their own husbands. Then came Lord Gormon Peake; Lords Smallwood, and Shawney; various lesser lords and landed knights. Amongst them Dunk glimpsed John the Fiddler and Alyn Cockshaw. Lord Alyn looked to be in his cups, though the feast had not yet properly begun.

By the time all of them had sauntered to the dais, the high table was as crowded as the benches. Lord Butterwell and his bride sat on plump downy cushions in a double throne of gilded oak. The rest planted themselves in tall chairs with fancifully carved arms. On the wall behind them, two huge banners hung from the rafters: the twin towers of Frey, blue on grey, and the green and white and yellow undy of the Butterwells.

It fell to Lord Frey to lead the toasts. "The king!" he began simply. Ser Glendon held his wine cup out above the water basin. Dunk clanked his cup against it, and against Ser Uthor's and the rest as well. They drank.

" Lord Butterwell, our gracious host," Frey proclaimed next. "May the Father grant him long life and many sons."

They drank again.

" Lady Butterwell, the maiden brick, my darling daughter. May the Mother make her fertile."

Frey gave the girl a smile. "I shall want a grandson before the year is out. Twins would suit me even better, so churn the butter well tonight, my sweet."

Laughter rang against the rafters, and the guests drank still once more. The wine was rich and red and sweet.

Then Lord Frey said, "I give you the King's Hand, Brynden Rivers. May the Crone's lamp light his path to wisdom." He lifted his goblet high and drank, together with Lord Butterwell and his bride and the others on the dais. Below the salt, Ser Glendon turned his cup over to spill its contents to the floor.

"A sad waste of good wine," said Maynard Plumm.

"I do not drink to kinslayers," said Ser Glendon. "Lord Bloodraven is a sorcerer and a bastard."

"Born bastard," Ser Uthor agreed mildly, "but his royal father made him legitimate as he lay dying." He drank deep, as did Ser Maynard and many others in the hall. Near as many lowered their cups, or turned them upside down as Ball had done. Dunk's own cup was heavy in his hand. How many eyes does Lord Bloodraven have? the riddle went. A thousand eyes, and one.

Toast followed toast, some proposed by Lord Frey and some by others. They drank to young Lord Tully, Lord Butterwell's liege lord, who had begged off from the wedding. They drank to the health of Leo Longthorn, Lord of Highgarden, who was rumored to be ailing. They drank to the memory of their gallant dead. Aye, thought Dunk, remembering. I'll gladly drink to them.

Ser John the Fiddler proposed the final toast. " To my brave brothers! I know that they are smiling tonight!"

Dunk had not intended to drink so much, with the jousting on the morrow, but the cups were filled anew after every toast, and he found he had a thirst. "Never refuse a cup of wine or a horn of ale," Ser Arlan had once told him, "it may be a year before you see another." It would have been discourteous not to toast the bride and groom, he told himself, and dangerous not to drink to the king and his Hand, with strangers all about.

Mercifully, the Fiddler's toast was the last. Lord Butterwell rose ponderously to thank them for coming and promise good jousting on the morrow. "Let the feast begin!"

Suckling pig was served at the high table, a peacock roasted in its plumage, a great pike crusted with crushed almonds. Not a bite of that made it down below the salt. Instead of suckling pig, they got salt pork, soaked in almond milk and peppered pleasantly. In place of peacock, they had capons, crisped up nice and brown and stuffed with onions, herbs, mushrooms, and roasted chestnuts. In place of pike, they ate chunks of flaky white cod in a pastry coffyn, with some sort of tasty brown sauce that Dunk could not quite place. There was pease porridge besides, buttered turnips, carrots drizzled with honey, and a ripe white cheese that smelled as strong as Bennis of the Brown Shield. Dunk ate well, but all the while wondered what Egg was getting in the yard. Just in case, he slipped half a capon into the pocket of his cloak, with some hunks of bread and a little of the smelly cheese.

As they ate, pipes and fiddles filled the air with spritely tunes, and the talk turned to the morrow's jousting. "Ser Franklyn Frey is well regarded along the Green Fork," said Uthor Underleaf, who seemed to know these local heroes well. "That's him upon the dais, the uncle of the bride. Lucas Nayland is down from Flag's Mire, he should not be discounted. Nor should Ser Mortimer Boggs, of Crackclaw Point. Elsewise, this should be a tourney of household knights and village heroes. Kirby Pimm and Galtry the Green are the best of those, though neither is a match for Lord Butterwell's good-son, Black Tom Heddle. A nasty bit of business, that one. He won the hand of His Lordship's eldest daughter by killing three of her other suitors, it's said, and once unhorsed the Lord of Casterly Rock."

"What, young Lord Tybolt?" asked Ser Maynard.

"No, the old Grey Lion, the one who died in the spring." That was how men spoke of those who had perished during the Great Spring Sickness. He died in the spring. Tens of thousands had died in the spring, among them a king and two young princes.

"Do not slight Ser Buford Bulwer," said Kyle the Cat. "The Old Ox slew forty men upon the Redgrass Field."

"And every year his count grows higher," said Ser Maynard. "Bulwer's day is done. Look at him.

Past sixty, soft and fat, and his right eye is good as blind."

"Do not trouble to search the hall for the champion," a voice behind Dunk said. "Here I stand, sers. Feast your eyes."

Dunk turned to find Ser John the Fiddler looming over him, a half smile on his lips. His white silk doublet had lagged sleeves lined with red satin, so long their points drooped down past his knees. A heavy silver chain looped across his chest, studded with huge dark amethysts whose color matched his eyes. That chain is worth as much as everything I own, Dunk thought.

The wine had colored Ser Glendon's cheeks and inflamed his pimples. "Who are you, to make such boasts?"

"They call me John the Fiddler."

"Are you a musician or a warrior?"

"I can make sweet song with either lance or resined bow, as it happens. Every wedding needs a singer, and every tourney needs a mystery knight. May I join you? Butterwell was good enough to place me on the dais, but I prefer the company of my fellow hedge knights to fat pink ladies and old men." The Fiddler clapped Dunk upon the shoulder. "Be a good fellow and shove over, Ser Duncan."

Dunk shoved over. "You are too late for food, ser."

"No matter. I know where Butterwell's kitchens are. There is still some wine, I trust?" The Fiddler smelled of oranges and limes, with a hint of some strange eastern spice beneath.

Nutmeg, perhaps. Dunk could not have said. What did he know of nutmeg?

"Your boasting is unseemly," Ser Glendon told the Fiddler.

"Truly? Then I must beg for your forgiveness, ser. I would never wish to give offense to any son of Fireball."

That took the youth aback. "You know who I am?"

"Your father's son, I hope."

"Look," said Ser Kyle the Cat. "The wedding pie."

Six kitchen boys were pushing it through the doors, upon a wide wheeled cart. The pie was brown and crusty and immense, and there were noises coming from inside it, squeaks and squawks and thumps. Lord and Lady Butterwell descended from the dais to meet it, sword in hand. When they cut it open, half a hundred birds burst forth to fly around the hall. In other wedding feasts Dunk had attended, the pies had been filled with doves or songbirds, but inside this one were bluejays and skylarks, pigeons and doves, mockingbirds and nightingales, small brown sparrows and a great red parrot. "One-and-twenty sorts of birds," said Ser Kyle.

"One-and-twenty sorts of bird droppings," said Ser Maynard.

"You have no poetry in your heart, ser."

"You have shit upon your shoulder."

"This is the proper way to fill a pie," Ser Kyle sniffed, cleaning off his tunic. "The pie is meant to be the marriage, and a true marriage has in it many sorts of things. . .joy and grief, pain and pleasure, love and lust and loyalty. So it is fitting that there be birds of many sorts. No man ever truly knows what a new wife will bring him."

"Her cunt," said Plumm, "or what would be the point?"

Dunk shoved back from the table. "I need a breath of air." It was a piss he needed, truth be told, but in fine company like this, it was more courteous to talk of air. "Pray excuse me."

"Flurry back, ser," said the Fiddler. "There are jugglers yet to come, and you do not want to miss the bedding."

1	# TMK — Dunk 1
2	
3	## Chapter Metadata
4	- **book:** TMK
5	- **chapter_number:** 1
6	- **pov_character:** Dunk
7	- **pov_chapter_number:** Dunk I
8	- **first_available:** pre-agot
9	- **location_primary:** Lakeshore inn and the weirwood-grove camp beside the lake (the ferry crossing)
10	- **location_secondary:** The inn's stable and yard; the ferry landing; (mentioned) Whitewalls, Winterfell, Summerhall, Tyrosh, Stoney Sept, Bitterbridge, Tumbler's Falls, Redgrass Field, the Arbor, Dorne, Oldtown
11	- **approximate_timeline:** Evening into night; unit part 2 of 9. Dunk and Egg reach the lakeshore inn at sundown, learn they have missed the day's last ferry, are refused food and lodging by the innkeep, and make camp for the night among the hedge knights in the old weirwood grove while awaiting the morning ferry.
12	- **time_markers:** "The sun was low in the west"; "The sun's going down"; "He'll be back first thing in the morning"; "the sun was a red memory in the west"; "We will be here until midmorning"; "As dusk deepened"; "The last dragon had perished half a century ago"; Glendon knighted "Half a year when the moon turns"; Butterwell "was once the King's Hand, as his father's father was before him"; "When the Black Dragon rose"; "Both perished on the Redgrass Field"; "his youngest died in the spring"; "Quentyn Ball died ... before I was born"; "In King Daeron's day"; "the Backfire Rebellion"; Glendon "Sixteen, might be. No more than eighteen"; Egg "a boy of eleven years"
13	- **chapter_summary:** Dunk and Egg reach a large lakeshore inn at sundown but have missed the day's last ferry, run by a man named Ned, and the innkeep — full up with Lord Shawney's and Lord Costayne's retinues — refuses to sell them the roast boar or give them a bed. She directs them to hedge knights camped by the stumps down the shore. In an old weirwood grove Dunk and Egg join Ser Kyle the Cat of Misty Moor, Ser Maynard Plumm, and the young Ser Glendon Ball, sharing salt beef, apples, pickled eggs, onions and sour wine. Over the fire the men discuss the coming wedding of Lord Butterwell to a Frey at Whitewalls, where a dragon's egg is the champion's prize, and trade talk of the realm's troubles — the ironmen's raids, King Aerys, the princes, and the Hand, Bloodraven. Glendon reveals himself as the son of Fireball (Quentyn Ball), who fought for the Black Dragon, and vows to claim the dragon's egg at Whitewalls.
14	- **unit_part:** 2 of 9
15	
16	## Physical Environment
17	- **Weather:** Warm; midges and flies swarm off the lake at dusk
18	- **Season indicators:** Summer implied by warmth and biting insects; "his youngest died in the spring" (recalled)
19	- **Time of day:** Late afternoon into full night (sundown, then dusk, then dark)
20	- **Lighting:** The setting sun turns the lake "red and gold, bright as a sheet of beaten copper"; "the sun was a red memory in the west"; firelight in the grove; Egg's shaven head "shone redly in the firelight"; steel glimmering in the firelight
21	- **Sounds:** Good smell aside — the innkeep's broom sweeping; the door closing "with a solid thump"; the soft crackle of the cook fire; the whetstone on Glendon's sword
22	- **Smells:** A good smell of roasting wild boar drifting from the inn windows ("made Dunk's mouth water"); the roast "crackling on its spit and dripping fat"; sour wine; salt beef
23	- **Notable sensory details:** The lake glimmering red and gold like beaten copper; flies and stinging midges rising off the lake at dusk (flies plague the horses, midges bite men); the only relief is sitting in the fire's smoke ("Cook or be devoured"); midges crawling on the back of Dunk's neck; the ring of white weirwood stumps and bone-pale roots in the grove

142	## Events & Actions
143	1. **Arrival at the lakeshore inn** — At sundown Dunk and Egg see the lake and the inn's turrets; Dunk dons his sweaty tunic and washes the road dust from his face to look "less the wild robber knight."
144	   - Agent: Dunk
145	   - Location: The lakeshore / inn approach
146	2. **Egg sees to the animals** — Dunk tells Egg to water the animals but not let them drink too much; Egg tends them at the yard's well and trough.
147	   - Agent: Egg
148	   - Patient: The horses and mule
149	   - Instigator: Dunk
150	   - Location: The inn's stable yard
151	3. **Dunk bargains with the innkeep** — Dunk learns the day's last ferry (run by Ned) is gone until morning, that the fares have risen (three pennies each, ten per horse and mule — "six-and-thirty"), and asks to buy boar and a place to sleep.
152	   - Agent: Dunk
153	   - Patient: Innkeep
154	   - Location: The inn steps
155	   - Outcome: Refused
156	4. **The innkeep refuses and shoos Dunk off** — She has no bed (full of lords' retinues) and reserves the boar and ale for her guests, sweeping her broom at Dunk and directing him to the hedge knights "down by the stumps."
157	   - Agent: Innkeep
158	   - Patient: Dunk
159	   - Instrument: Her broom
160	   - Location: The inn steps
161	   - Outcome: Dunk sent away; door shut "with a solid thump"
162	5. **Egg soaks the salt beef** — Dunk finds Egg soaking his feet in the trough, slings him the greathelm, and orders him to draw well water and soak the salt beef (not in the foot-trough).
163	   - Agent: Egg
164	   - Instrument: Dunk's greathelm; the well
165	   - Instigator: Dunk
166	   - Location: The stable yard
167	6. **Finding the hedge knights' camp** — Egg spies the fire in the woods along the lakeshore; they lead the animals to it and reach the old weirwood grove.
168	   - Agent: Egg (spots the fire); Dunk and Egg (travel)
169	   - Location: Woods along the lakeshore / weirwood grove
170	7. **Introductions at the fire** — Dunk hails the armed men openly, names himself Ser Duncan the Tall and "the lad is Egg," and asks to share their fire; Ser Kyle greets them and names Ser Glendon Ball and Ser Maynard Plumm.
171	   - Agent: Dunk; Ser Kyle
172	   - Patient: Ser Kyle, Ser Maynard, Ser Glendon (met)
173	   - Location: The weirwood grove
174	   - Outcome: Welcomed to the fire
175	8. **Egg conceals his identity** — Asked his odd name and his baldness, Egg withholds that "Egg" is short for Aegon and gives the shaven-for-spurs story rather than the usual "rootworms" tale.
176	   - Agent: Egg
177	   - Patient: Ser Kyle (and the camp)
178	   - Outcome: Identity kept hidden
179	9. **Unsaddling and provisioning the animals** — Dunk and Egg unsaddle, feed, water, and hobble Thunder, Rain, and Maester for the night.
180	   - Agent: Dunk and Egg
181	   - Patient: Thunder, Rain, Maester
182	   - Location: The grove
183	10. **The shared meal and fireside talk** — The men pool salt beef, apples, pickled eggs, onions, and wine and discuss the Whitewalls wedding, the dragon's-egg prize, the ironmen's raids, and the realm's rulers.
184	    - Agent: Dunk, Egg, Ser Kyle, Ser Maynard, Ser Glendon
185	    - Location: The grove camp
186	11. **Egg speaks out about Prince Maekar; Dunk silences him** — When Kyle says Rhaegel's twins may die "of Maekar's mace," Egg protests loudly that Maekar loves his brother; Dunk growls him quiet and sets him to passing out the beef.
187	    - Agent: Egg (speaks); Dunk (silences)
188	    - Patient: Egg
189	    - Instigator: Dunk
190	    - Location: The grove camp
191	    - Outcome: Egg falls sullenly silent
192	12. **Ser Kyle names Blackfyre men "traitors"; Glendon takes offense** — Kyle calls the Blackfyre men traitors while telling of the Backfire Rebellion; Glendon, blade in hand and pock-marks reddening, declares his father fought for the Black Dragon and that Daemon was no traitor.
193	    - Agent: Ser Kyle (gives offense); Ser Glendon (reacts)
194	    - Patient: Ser Glendon
195	    - Instrument: Ser Glendon's drawn sword
196	    - Witness: Dunk
197	    - Location: The grove camp
198	    - Outcome: Tension; Dunk defuses it
199	13. **Glendon reveals himself as Fireball's son** — Glendon turns his shield to show the fireball sigil, says "I come from hero's blood"; Egg names him "Fireball's son," and Glendon confirms he is Quentyn Ball's son, vowing to claim the dragon's egg at Whitewalls.
200	    - Agent: Ser Glendon (reveals); Egg (names him)
201	    - Instrument: Glendon's fireball shield
202	    - Witness: Dunk, Ser Kyle, Ser Maynard
203	    - Location: The grove camp
204	    - Outcome: Glendon's parentage revealed; he smiles for the first time

274	## POV Character's Internal State
275	- **Emotional state:** Weary and travel-worn, hungry (his mouth waters at the boar; he sucks salt beef and tries "not to think about the roast boar"); glum; wary of armed strangers; amused by Egg ("trying not to smile"); increasingly anxious as the fireside talk turns to treason and to Egg's family.
276	- **Primary preoccupation:** Getting food and a cheap place to sleep, and keeping Egg quiet and his identity hidden; concern over the ferry cost ("more than he had hoped to spend").
277	- **Key decisions made:** To camp with the hedge knights; to soak the salt beef; to keep his and Egg's business vague ("We net them on the road, is all"); to hold to the northward journey to Winterfell rather than divert to Whitewalls; to defuse the Red/Black quarrel; to silence Egg.
278	- **Self-deception flags:** "Perhaps he's finally learned to hold that tongue of his" — immediately disproved when Egg speaks out about Prince Maekar; "Red or Black? was not a thing you asked a man" — reflecting on trouble he then has to smooth over.

# TMK — Dunk 1

## Chapter Metadata
- **book:** TMK
- **chapter_number:** 1
- **pov_character:** Dunk
- **pov_chapter_number:** Dunk I
- **first_available:** pre-agot
- **location_primary:** Lakeshore inn and the weirwood-grove camp beside the lake (the ferry crossing)
- **location_secondary:** The inn's stable and yard; the ferry landing; (mentioned) Whitewalls, Winterfell, Summerhall, Tyrosh, Stoney Sept, Bitterbridge, Tumbler's Falls, Redgrass Field, the Arbor, Dorne, Oldtown
- **approximate_timeline:** Evening into night; unit part 2 of 9. Dunk and Egg reach the lakeshore inn at sundown, learn they have missed the day's last ferry, are refused food and lodging by the innkeep, and make camp for the night among the hedge knights in the old weirwood grove while awaiting the morning ferry.
- **time_markers:** "The sun was low in the west"; "The sun's going down"; "He'll be back first thing in the morning"; "the sun was a red memory in the west"; "We will be here until midmorning"; "As dusk deepened"; "The last dragon had perished half a century ago"; Glendon knighted "Half a year when the moon turns"; Butterwell "was once the King's Hand, as his father's father was before him"; "When the Black Dragon rose"; "Both perished on the Redgrass Field"; "his youngest died in the spring"; "Quentyn Ball died ... before I was born"; "In King Daeron's day"; "the Backfire Rebellion"; Glendon "Sixteen, might be. No more than eighteen"; Egg "a boy of eleven years"
- **chapter_summary:** Dunk and Egg reach a large lakeshore inn at sundown but have missed the day's last ferry, run by a man named Ned, and the innkeep — full up with Lord Shawney's and Lord Costayne's retinues — refuses to sell them the roast boar or give them a bed. She directs them to hedge knights camped by the stumps down the shore. In an old weirwood grove Dunk and Egg join Ser Kyle the Cat of Misty Moor, Ser Maynard Plumm, and the young Ser Glendon Ball, sharing salt beef, apples, pickled eggs, onions and sour wine. Over the fire the men discuss the coming wedding of Lord Butterwell to a Frey at Whitewalls, where a dragon's egg is the champion's prize, and trade talk of the realm's troubles — the ironmen's raids, King Aerys, the princes, and the Hand, Bloodraven. Glendon reveals himself as the son of Fireball (Quentyn Ball), who fought for the Black Dragon, and vows to claim the dragon's egg at Whitewalls.
- **unit_part:** 2 of 9

## Physical Environment
- **Weather:** Warm; midges and flies swarm off the lake at dusk
- **Season indicators:** Summer implied by warmth and biting insects; "his youngest died in the spring" (recalled)
- **Time of day:** Late afternoon into full night (sundown, then dusk, then dark)
- **Lighting:** The setting sun turns the lake "red and gold, bright as a sheet of beaten copper"; "the sun was a red memory in the west"; firelight in the grove; Egg's shaven head "shone redly in the firelight"; steel glimmering in the firelight
- **Sounds:** Good smell aside — the innkeep's broom sweeping; the door closing "with a solid thump"; the soft crackle of the cook fire; the whetstone on Glendon's sword
- **Smells:** A good smell of roasting wild boar drifting from the inn windows ("made Dunk's mouth water"); the roast "crackling on its spit and dripping fat"; sour wine; salt beef
- **Notable sensory details:** The lake glimmering red and gold like beaten copper; flies and stinging midges rising off the lake at dusk (flies plague the horses, midges bite men); the only relief is sitting in the fire's smoke ("Cook or be devoured"); midges crawling on the back of Dunk's neck; the ring of white weirwood stumps and bone-pale roots in the grove

## Characters Present
| Character | Role in Chapter | First Appearance? | Notes |
|-----------|----------------|-------------------|-------|
| Dunk (Ser Duncan the Tall) | POV; hedge knight seeking food and lodging, then camps with the hedge knights | (uncertain — verify) | Very large; scar on cheek; late master was Ser Arlan; bound north to Winterfell |
| Egg | Dunk's squire; tends animals, soaks the salt beef, speaks out of turn | (uncertain — verify) | Shaven head; eleven years old; "Egg" is short for Aegon; son of Prince Maekar |
| Innkeep (unnamed woman) | Runs the lakeshore inn; refuses Dunk food and a bed, directs him to the hedge knights | Yes | Full up with Lord Shawney's and Lord Costayne's retinues; shoos Dunk with her broom |
| Ser Kyle, the Cat of Misty Moor | Hedge knight at the camp; talkative, seeks service at Whitewalls | Yes | Stout, middling years; ginger whiskers; once carved a wooden sword for young Joffrey Caswell |
| Ser Maynard Plumm | Hedge knight at the camp | Yes | Tall, thin, stoop-shouldered; distant kin to Lord Viserys Plumm |
| Ser Glendon Ball | Young hedge knight at the camp; reveals he is Fireball's son | Yes | Short, chunky; ~16–18; means to claim the dragon's egg at Whitewalls |

| Egg | Shaven/bald head ("shaven head shone redly in the firelight") | — | — | — | Big floppy hat | — | Bald; "Egg" is short for Aegon | Eleven years |
| Ser Kyle, the Cat of Misty Moor | Flamboyant ginger whiskers framing his face | — | Stout, of middling years | — | Tattered finery | — | — | Middling years |
| Ser Maynard Plumm | Long straight flaxen hair | — | Tall, thin, stoop-shouldered | — | Purple cloak (frayed, badly dyed) fastened with a moonstone brooch big as a hen's egg; dun-colored roughspun and stained brown leather | — | Moonstone brooch | — |
| Ser Glendon Ball | Tufts of dark brown hair beneath his halfhelm | Small, close-set eyes | Short and chunky; thick shoulders, muscular arms | Pimples/pock-marks on his cheeks ("in place of whiskers"); pock-marks "flamed as red as open sores" | Good cloth but well-worn, ill-matched hand-me-downs; iron halfhelm | Sword (whetted at the fire); shield bearing a fireball; scabbard | Shaggy eyebrows ("two caterpillars after a wet spring"); bulbous nose; pugnacious chin | "Sixteen, might be. No more than eighteen"; a knight "Half a year when the moon turns" |

| The hedge knights' shared camp meal | Salt beef (Dunk & Egg); a bag of apples (Maynard); pickled eggs and onions (Kyle) | Skin of sour, strong wine passed hand to hand | Dunk, Egg, Ser Kyle, Ser Maynard, Ser Glendon | The weirwood-grove camp | Called by Kyle "the makings of a feast"; wine "sour and strong"; "Even sour wine is better than none" |
| Anticipated wedding feast at Whitewalls | — | Butterwell "said to have the best wines north of the Arbor"; finer vintages | (anticipated) the wedding guests | Whitewalls (mentioned) | Singers, musicians, jugglers, tumblers, a troupe of comic dwarfs promised |

| Event | Type | Host | Guest(s) | Details |
|-------|------|------|----------|---------|
| Innkeep refuses Dunk food and lodging | Denied hospitality (paid) | Innkeep | Dunk (and Egg) | No bed to offer (full of Shawney's and Costayne's men); the boar and ale reserved "for my guests"; will not "run short of food or drink before they were surfeit"; sends Dunk off with her broom |
| Sharing the hedge knights' fire | Guest-fire sharing among hedge knights | Ser Kyle, Ser Maynard, Ser Glendon | Dunk and Egg | Dunk asks "May we share your fire?" and hails them openly ("It was never wise to take armed men unawares"); welcomed by Ser Kyle ("most welcome ... as is your lad"); food pooled into a shared meal |
| Anticipated wedding feast at Whitewalls | Wedding hospitality | Lord Butterwell | Attending lords, hedge knights, tourney entrants | Kyle urges Dunk to come for the feast and possible service; entertainment promised |

## Events & Actions
1. **Arrival at the lakeshore inn** — At sundown Dunk and Egg see the lake and the inn's turrets; Dunk dons his sweaty tunic and washes the road dust from his face to look "less the wild robber knight."
   - Agent: Dunk
   - Location: The lakeshore / inn approach
2. **Egg sees to the animals** — Dunk tells Egg to water the animals but not let them drink too much; Egg tends them at the yard's well and trough.
   - Agent: Egg
   - Patient: The horses and mule
   - Instigator: Dunk
   - Location: The inn's stable yard
3. **Dunk bargains with the innkeep** — Dunk learns the day's last ferry (run by Ned) is gone until morning, that the fares have risen (three pennies each, ten per horse and mule — "six-and-thirty"), and asks to buy boar and a place to sleep.
   - Agent: Dunk
   - Patient: Innkeep
   - Location: The inn steps
   - Outcome: Refused
4. **The innkeep refuses and shoos Dunk off** — She has no bed (full of lords' retinues) and reserves the boar and ale for her guests, sweeping her broom at Dunk and directing him to the hedge knights "down by the stumps."
   - Agent: Innkeep
   - Patient: Dunk
   - Instrument: Her broom
   - Location: The inn steps
   - Outcome: Dunk sent away; door shut "with a solid thump"
5. **Egg soaks the salt beef** — Dunk finds Egg soaking his feet in the trough, slings him the greathelm, and orders him to draw well water and soak the salt beef (not in the foot-trough).
   - Agent: Egg
   - Instrument: Dunk's greathelm; the well
   - Instigator: Dunk
   - Location: The stable yard
6. **Finding the hedge knights' camp** — Egg spies the fire in the woods along the lakeshore; they lead the animals to it and reach the old weirwood grove.
   - Agent: Egg (spots the fire); Dunk and Egg (travel)
   - Location: Woods along the lakeshore / weirwood grove
7. **Introductions at the fire** — Dunk hails the armed men openly, names himself Ser Duncan the Tall and "the lad is Egg," and asks to share their fire; Ser Kyle greets them and names Ser Glendon Ball and Ser Maynard Plumm.
   - Agent: Dunk; Ser Kyle
   - Patient: Ser Kyle, Ser Maynard, Ser Glendon (met)
   - Location: The weirwood grove
   - Outcome: Welcomed to the fire
8. **Egg conceals his identity** — Asked his odd name and his baldness, Egg withholds that "Egg" is short for Aegon and gives the shaven-for-spurs story rather than the usual "rootworms" tale.
   - Agent: Egg
   - Patient: Ser Kyle (and the camp)
   - Outcome: Identity kept hidden
9. **Unsaddling and provisioning the animals** — Dunk and Egg unsaddle, feed, water, and hobble Thunder, Rain, and Maester for the night.
   - Agent: Dunk and Egg
   - Patient: Thunder, Rain, Maester
   - Location: The grove
10. **The shared meal and fireside talk** — The men pool salt beef, apples, pickled eggs, onions, and wine and discuss the Whitewalls wedding, the dragon's-egg prize, the ironmen's raids, and the realm's rulers.
    - Agent: Dunk, Egg, Ser Kyle, Ser Maynard, Ser Glendon
    - Location: The grove camp
11. **Egg speaks out about Prince Maekar; Dunk silences him** — When Kyle says Rhaegel's twins may die "of Maekar's mace," Egg protests loudly that Maekar loves his brother; Dunk growls him quiet and sets him to passing out the beef.
    - Agent: Egg (speaks); Dunk (silences)
    - Patient: Egg
    - Instigator: Dunk
    - Location: The grove camp
    - Outcome: Egg falls sullenly silent
12. **Ser Kyle names Blackfyre men "traitors"; Glendon takes offense** — Kyle calls the Blackfyre men traitors while telling of the Backfire Rebellion; Glendon, blade in hand and pock-marks reddening, declares his father fought for the Black Dragon and that Daemon was no traitor.
    - Agent: Ser Kyle (gives offense); Ser Glendon (reacts)
    - Patient: Ser Glendon
    - Instrument: Ser Glendon's drawn sword
    - Witness: Dunk
    - Location: The grove camp
    - Outcome: Tension; Dunk defuses it
13. **Glendon reveals himself as Fireball's son** — Glendon turns his shield to show the fireball sigil, says "I come from hero's blood"; Egg names him "Fireball's son," and Glendon confirms he is Quentyn Ball's son, vowing to claim the dragon's egg at Whitewalls.
    - Agent: Ser Glendon (reveals); Egg (names him)
    - Instrument: Glendon's fireball shield
    - Witness: Dunk, Ser Kyle, Ser Maynard
    - Location: The grove camp
    - Outcome: Glendon's parentage revealed; he smiles for the first time

## Spatial Layout & Movement
| Phase | Who | Position / Movement | Relative To | Notes |
|-------|-----|---------------------|-------------|-------|
| Arrival | Dunk, Egg | Reach the lakeshore, then the inn | The lake and inn turrets above the willows | Sun low in the west |
| Arrival | Dunk | Goes to the inn steps to ask for food | The inn | Egg left to tend animals at the yard |
| Departure | Dunk | Turned away from the inn | The inn steps | Directed to the hedge knights "down by the stumps" |
| Advance | Dunk, Egg | Lead the animals toward the firelight in the woods | Along the lakeshore | Egg carries the beef-filled helm, "sloshing with each step" |
| Arrival | Dunk, Egg | Enter the weirwood grove | The cook fire and the two seated men | Glendon sits apart against a chestnut tree |
| Assembly | Dunk, Egg, Kyle, Maynard, Glendon | Seated on stumps around the fire | The cook fire | Sit close to the fire's smoke to escape the midges |
| Confrontation | Ser Glendon | Blade in hand, sinews taut, facing Kyle | Ser Kyle across the fire | Over the "traitors"/Black Dragon exchange |

| King Aerys reigns and is weak ("hides amongst his books"); Bloodraven put him on the throne | Ser Kyle | The campmates | No |
| Prince Rhaegel is feeble-minded, comes next in line to Aerys (before Maekar), and has twins/children | Ser Kyle; Ser Maynard | The campmates | No |
| Prince Maekar broods at Summerhall and is Prince Rhaegel's brother who "loves him well" | Ser Kyle; Egg | The campmates | No |
| Prince Maekar is Egg's father | Narration (Egg ignores "the mention of his father's name") | Dunk, Egg | Reader (within this unit) |
| Bloodraven is the King's Hand ("the white worm"); his eye is fixed on Tyrosh; he keeps the king's ships close to stop Bittersteel and the Blackfyre sons from crossing | Ser Kyle; Ser Maynard | The campmates | No |
| Bittersteel sits in exile at Tyrosh, plotting with the sons of Daemon Blackfyre | Ser Maynard | The campmates | No |
| Kyle predicts that when Aerys dies there will be war between Lord Rivers (Bloodraven) and Prince Maekar — "the Hand against the heir" | Ser Kyle | The campmates | No |
| Bloodraven is also called Lord Rivers | Ser Kyle's phrasing | The campmates, Dunk | Reader (within this unit) |
| Dunk squired for a knight who fought with the Red Dragon and later served another who fought for the Black | Dunk | The campmates | No |
| Kyle once saved the life of the Lord of Bitterbridge (Lord Armond) during the Backfire Rebellion and once had his sword sworn to Joffrey Caswell's father | Ser Kyle | The campmates | No |

## Dialogue of Note
| Speaker | Listener | Quote/Paraphrase | Context |
|---------|----------|------------------|---------|
| Innkeep | Dunk | "Are you come for the ferry? You're too late. The sun's going down, and Ned don't like to cross by night unless the moon is full." | Turning Dunk away at the inn steps |
| Innkeep | Dunk | "The stables are for horses. That's why we call them stables. You're big as a horse, I'll grant you, but I see only two legs." | Refusing Dunk a place to sleep |
| Innkeep | Dunk | "I can't be expected to feed all the Seven Kingdoms. The boar is for my guests." | Reserving food for the lords |
| Egg | Dunk | "Can I please eat my boots instead, ser? I'll make a new pair out of the salt beef. It's tougher." | Complaining about the salt beef vs. the boar |
| Egg | Ser Kyle | "A short one, ser." | Deflecting the question of what "Egg" is short for |
| Egg | Ser Kyle | "I shaved it off, ser. I mean to stay shaven until I earn my spurs." | Cover story for his bald head |
| Ser Kyle | Dunk | "I am Ser Kyle, the Cat of Misty Moor. Under yonder chestnut sits Ser Glendon, ah, Ball. And here you have the good Ser Maynard Plumm." | Introductions |
| Ser Maynard | The camp | "One might say that he is of the sweet Plumms, whilst I am of the sour." | On his distant kinship to Lord Viserys Plumm |
| Ser Maynard | The camp | "When the Black Dragon rose, this lord of cows sent one son to Daemon and one to Daeron, to make certain there was a Butterwell on the winning side." | On Butterwell hedging the Blackfyre rebellion |
| Ser Glendon | The camp | "The Warrior hates cravens." | Condemning Butterwell's hedging |
| Ser Glendon | The camp | "I mean to make my name at Whitewalls, and claim the dragon's egg." | Declaring his purpose |
| Ser Kyle | Dunk | "Bloodraven is the root of all our woes, the white worm gnawing at the heart of the realm." | Blaming the Hand |
| Dunk | Ser Kyle | "Words like that can cost a man his head. Some might say you're talking treason." | Warning Kyle |
| Ser Kyle | Dunk | "How can the truth be treason? ... when [Aerys] dies, it will be bloody war between Lord Rivers and Prince Maekar for the crown, the Hand against the heir." | Predicting the succession war |
| Egg | The camp | "Prince Maekar is Prince Rhaegel's brother. He loves him well. He'd never do harm to him or his." | Defending his father |
| Dunk | Egg | "Be quiet, boy. These knights want none of your opinions." | Silencing Egg |
| Ser Glendon | Ser Kyle | "My father fought for the Black Dragon." | Reacting to "traitors" |
| Ser Glendon | The camp | "Daemon Blackfyre was no traitor. The old king gave him the sword. ... Daemon was the better man." | Defending Daemon Blackfyre |
| Egg | Ser Glendon | "You're Fireball's son." | Identifying Glendon |
| Ser Glendon | The camp | "Quentyn Ball died ... before I was born, but in me, he lives again." | Confirming his parentage |

## POV Character's Internal State
- **Emotional state:** Weary and travel-worn, hungry (his mouth waters at the boar; he sucks salt beef and tries "not to think about the roast boar"); glum; wary of armed strangers; amused by Egg ("trying not to smile"); increasingly anxious as the fireside talk turns to treason and to Egg's family.
- **Primary preoccupation:** Getting food and a cheap place to sleep, and keeping Egg quiet and his identity hidden; concern over the ferry cost ("more than he had hoped to spend").
- **Key decisions made:** To camp with the hedge knights; to soak the salt beef; to keep his and Egg's business vague ("We net them on the road, is all"); to hold to the northward journey to Winterfell rather than divert to Whitewalls; to defuse the Red/Black quarrel; to silence Egg.
- **Self-deception flags:** "Perhaps he's finally learned to hold that tongue of his" — immediately disproved when Egg speaks out about Prince Maekar; "Red or Black? was not a thing you asked a man" — reflecting on trouble he then has to smooth over.
